items.title: Contrary to JAVA, embedded in Python is the idea of Methods and Functions
items.author: Onaolapo-11
items.content:
Day 70 [November 15, 2025]

I need to buckle down, as I'm still lagging on day day 3 & 4 goals, "Day 3-4: Control structures (if-else, loops)", as well as day 5 (and 6) goals, "Day 5-6: Functions and modules", and Day 7 target (exercises) (Meta AI, personal communication, August 8, 2025). If I haven't covered this, I can't make progress on day 8 - 69 goals.

Goals: As extracted from the 'Python for Software Development' textbook by Halvorsen (n.d.):

  • Creating Functions in Python - Introduction
  • Functions with multiple return values
  • Creating Classes in Python

Notes: Python for Data Science, AI & Development Course (IBM) (Santarcangelo, n.d.):

Module 2: Python Data Structures

    While JAVA and Python are Object-oriented Programming languages, contrary to JAVA, embedded in Python is the idea of Methods and Functions. JAVA only utilizes the idea of Methods (geeksforgeeks, 2025).

    Summary: Contrary to JAVA, embedded in Python is the idea of Methods and Functions.

items.title: Brand Tagging with VLMs
items.author: Ville Vesilehto
items.content:
Build a two-stage logo pipeline:

  1. Retrieval - generate image embeddings for small crops and match against a logo dictionary with FAISS cosine search. Use SigLIP-2 (NaFlex) so logos are not distorted and small marks still pop.

  2. Verification - for top matches, ask LLaVA-OneVision-1.5 a strict JSON question ("Is this the X logo?") and accept only high-confidence "yes". It's a good model sir.

A bit longer post this time around.

Brand tagging in real-world video is hard: logos are tiny, partly occluded, moving, and often appear on textured backgrounds. A practical approach is a two-stage pipeline:

  • First retrieve likely logo crops with a fast contrastive image encoder.
  • Then verify each candidate with a vision-language model (VLM) that can read text and reason about shapes and context. Each candidate is a (frame, bbox, brand, retrieval_score) record that we pass to the VLM.

This tutorial combines a modern image-text encoder (SigLIP-2, NaFlex variant) for high-recall retrieval with a VLM from the LLaVA family for precise, structured yes/no verification.

You could train a YOLO-style detector for specific brands. This post, however, focuses on a more flexible "embedding + VLM" approach that adapts quickly to new logos without retraining. Some might find this fun.

Contrastive encoders such as CLIP/SigLIP produce embeddings where similar visuals are close. FAISS makes nearest-neighbor search over many references instantaneous. You can treat logo search as nearest-neighbor lookup instead of training a custom detector.

Verification reduces false positives. A VLM can explicitly answer "Is this the Red Bull logo?" and justify the decision, improving precision on lookalikes, partial views, and blur.

Pointers to the underlying research for the initiated:

You’ll need to be comfortable with basic Python and PyTorch. No prior experience with FAISS or VLMs required.

Hardware/software requirements:

  • At least one NVIDIA GPU (at least 32 GB VRAM recommended)
  • CUDA 12.x, Python 3.10–3.12
  • Disk: 5–10 GB (models + caches)

This tutorial was built and tested on a single NVIDIA H200.

Prepare the Python environment as follows:

python3 -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -U torch torchvision torchaudio \
  "transformers>=4.45" accelerate pillow opencv-python faiss-cpu \
  numpy pydantic polars

For simplicity we'll use ƒaiss-cpu as it's fine at this scale. It's a single logo after all. GPU for the models.

Example video (Creative Commons):

  • Title: Red Bull Racing Pit Stop Practice (2015), 44 s, 1920x1080.
  • License: CC BY-SA 4.0 - attribute ProtoplasmaKid / Wikimedia Commons.
  • It has tiny, moving logos, occlusion, uniforms, car bodywork, pit rig and acts as a great stress-test.
frames (2–4 FPS) ──▶ crops (multi-scale grid)
         │
         ▼
 [Stage 1] Retrieval (SigLIP-2 image features + FAISS cosine)
         │      └─ top-K per frame/brand with heuristics
         ▼
 [Stage 2] VLM verification (LLaVA-OneVision-1.5 JSON verdict)
         │
         ▼
    JSONL evidence

Models used in this post:

SigLIP-2 reports better zero-shot and retrieval performance than prior SigLIP/CLIP models on public benchmarks. NaFlex means the encoder resizes each crop to a grid of flexible patches instead of forcing a fixed square, so long thin logos don't get squashed.

OneVision-1.5 is an open VLM family. Its card reports strong benchmark leads vs other open models.

Both are Apache-2.0 licensed.

wget https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/ba/Red_Bull_Racing_Pit_Stop_Practice.webm

Then extract frames. Two FPS, scaled down to 1280x720:

 frames
ffmpeg :v 3 frames/f_%06d.jpg

This yields 88 JPG files.

2) Prepare a logo dictionary

In this example we're only interested in the Red Bull logo. Create a directory you want to use. Grab the logo:

 logos
wget  logos https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/fi/a/a5/Red_Bull_logo.png

This tutorial is for educational purposes only and is not affiliated with or endorsed by Red Bull. Red Bull is a registered trademark of Red Bull GmbH. It's a decent energy drink though.

3) Build the logo index (FAISS, SigLIP-2)

Save to :

SigLIP-2 provides get_image_features and NaFlex dynamic resizing to minimize distortion on non-square inputs — useful for narrow/wide logos.

python build_logo_index.py
...
Indexed 1 brands into artifacts/logos.faiss

￼ 4) Generate crops + Stage-1 retrieval

Save the following to :

This is quite a few things so breaking it down:

  • Crops: multi-scale grid over each frame using and , yielding square patches and their tuples.
  • Embeddings: SigLIP‑2 on each crop batch, then L2‑normalize features.
  • Search: FAISS over the logo index; uses inner‑product on normalized vectors (cosine). If the index is L2, we convert to cosine ().
  • Types: FAISS expects contiguous ; embeddings are cast/contiguous before .
  • Thresholds: keep top‑ per crop, then filter by . You can override at runtime:
    • TOPK=10 COSINE_TH=0.65 python retrieve.py
  • Debugging:
    • writes every raw match to (ranked with scores).
    • also saves artifacts/debug_vis/*_debug.jpg with [brand score#rank] boxes.
    • Optional: (only draw ≥ threshold), (cap boxes).
  • Outputs: filtered candidates land in and are fed to the verifier stage.
  • Knobs to tune recall/precision: crop sizes, stride, , . For higher recall, increase sizes or ; for precision, raise the threshold and later add a margin or temporal smoothing.
$ python retrieve.py
[retrieve] index ntotal=1, brands=1
[retrieve] frames found=88
wrote 25 retrieval candidates → candidates.jsonl

Depending on the source material this probably needs tuning for recall/precision. The debugging knobs are quite nice. Here's an example frame from the video showing how it looks like on frame #7:

Frame from Red Bull Racing Pit Stop Practice (2015), ProtoplasmaKid / Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA 4.0.

Save the following to :

  • Inputs: entries with , , , .
  • Crop: open the frame and per candidate.
  • Prompting: one system + one user message; user contains the target brand and an explicit "JSON only" schema.
  • Generation: , , for deterministic outputs.
  • Parsing: balanced-brace JSON extraction with smart-quote normalization; on failure returns . Set to dump raw text to .
  • Output: writes with , , merged onto each candidate.
  • Cost control: keep retrieval strict (lower , higher ) to limit VLM calls as that's the main runtime driver.
$ python verify.py
wrote detections.jsonl

And what does look like? It has items like this (one per line):

{
  "frame": "f_000007.jpg",
  "bbox": [
    384,
    288,
    192,
    192
  ],
  "score_retr": 0.7020304203033447,
  "brand": "Red_Bull_logo",
  "verdict": "yes",
  "confidence_vlm": 0.95,
  "rationale": "red bull charging bull silhouette, red and yellow colors"
}

Here 0.702 is the cosine similarity between the crop and the logo embedding.

I hope this showcases how a simple two-stage recipe - SigLIP-2 retrieval + VLM verification - can turn a semi-noisy video into reviewable brand evidence. I do want to mention that a pipeline like this is meant as a powerful filter, not an oracle. Human in the loop needed.

On this specific 44-second clip, with the thresholds above, I get N true positives, M false positives, and 0 missed clear logos (subjective visual check).

That being said, this is probably not a production-ready detector. We trade some accuracy and runtime for simplicity and transparency. Some improvement ideas I had in mind:

  • dtype could be replaced with lower precision, like .
  • Add a margin filter (top1 − top2 ≥ 0.15) and simple temporal smoothing. Confirm across 2–3 adjacent frames.
  • Add a lightweight OCR gate for texty marks to backstop retrieval.
  • Calibrate thresholds per brand, as some logos need higher or .
  • Expand the logo dictionary and try multi-resolution templates (flat vs curved surfaces).
  • Speed/scale: use FAISS IVF/PQ for larger dictionaries.
  • Quantize the VLM or batch crops.
  • Maybe consider a second encoder for consensus (SigLIP‑2 + CLIP) to reduce lookalikes.
  • For 4K frames, consider either down-scaling first or increasing STRIDE (e.g. 0.75) to avoid generating tens of thousands of crops per frame.

As always, credits: thanks to the SigLIP-2 authors & maintainers and the LLaVA-OneVision team for open releases. And attribution for the example video: ProtoplasmaKid / Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA 4.0. ￼

items.title: Krish Naik: Stop Fighting with Kubernetes! Scale Python to 1000s of Machines with Coiled
items.author: Vibe YouTube
items.content:
Coiled is a lightweight cloud compute platform built for Python data engineers and scientists. You never have to touch Docker or Kubernetes—just add one decorator (or a single line) and your code runs on hundreds of machines without leaving your usual workflow: pandas, NumPy, PyTorch and all your favorite tools still work out of the box.

It spins up VMs on demand and tears them down when you’re done, so you only pay for what you use. Plus, it automatically syncs your local environment, packages, credentials and files to the cloud—no more Docker builds or environment.yml headaches.

items.title: Python Core Utility - `collections.defaultdict`
items.author: Shodhan Save
items.content:
The is a subclass of the built-in that accepts a callable () during its initialization. Then, when you try to access a in the initialized object, it does a regular dict lookup to fetch the key's value. If the is missing, it calls the that generates a value for the requested. This key-value pair is then stored in the dict and the value is returned.

The primary use case is when you need to build up a dict based on an iterable and don't want to check if keys exist before operating on them. This is extremely common when grouping items, counting occurrences, or building nested data structures.

For example, when grouping items by some attribute, you'd normally need to check if the key exists before appending to a list. With , you can just append directly - if the key doesn't exist, it automatically creates an empty list first.

Another common scenario is counting: instead of checking if a key exists before incrementing a counter, you can use and increment directly, since missing keys default to .

The beauty of is that it eliminates boilerplate conditional logic, making your code cleaner and often more readable!

Here's a basic example showing the difference between regular dict and defaultdict:

A bit about dict internals and default_factory

In the standard implementation, when we do a lookup like , it calls the method. If this method cannot find the , it calls the method.

In a regular , this then raises a . In a however, it calls the callable (which is the first argument passed to as stated above). This callable is then executed, and its return value is assigned to and is also returned.

The can be any callable, including builtins, functions, lambdas, or even left blank. If or nothing is passed, then upon lookup for a non-existent key, a is raised, like a regular dict.

As this callable decides what is returned when a non-existent key is looked-ip for, it must be a callable that can be called without any arguments. Then, the return value of this callable is assigned to the key.

The agument can be any callable that returns a value. This can be used to demostrate various use cases, viz.:

  • To directly append into a list:

  • To count grouped objects:

  • Set custom default values:

  • Nested (2D) data structures:

Here are some practical examples where shines.

Grouping items by attribute

One of the most common uses - grouping a list of items by some property:

Building an inverted index

When working with search functionality, you often need to build an inverted index - mapping words to document IDs:

Counting occurrences with grouping

A scenario I encounter frequently - counting events or items with multiple dimensions:

Accumulating values for API response aggregation

When aggregating data from multiple API calls or database queries:

The method of does NOT use the default factory

This is probably the most surprising behavior. When you use on a , it behaves exactly like a regular dict - it does NOT call the default factory.

In case you aren't aware, the is the method of that is called when we do a lookup using square brackets - . So what this means is (where is a ) will call the , but will not call the :

Passing or nothing as default_factory

If you create a without a factory function (or with ), it behaves like a regular dict when accessing missing keys - you get a :

Note : This works because here we are setting a to a callable that just returns when a new key is requested for.

Comparing defaultdict with regular dict

Two s are equal each other and to a regular dict if they all have the same items, even if they have different :

This means, is not considered for equality check.

Calling on the dict does not trigger

The operator checks for key existence but does NOT trigger the default factory:

This actually makes sense - you don't want membership testing to have side effects!

Unexpected mutations during iteration

While iterating over a defaultdict, accessing non-existent keys during iteration triggers the . This is obvious, as we know that a lookup triggers method, which in turn, calls the that creates the entry.

items.title: Part 1: Mastering Dynamic Programming — 5 Steps to Solve It (Longest Increasing Subsequence Using Tabulation / Bottom-Up)
items.author: Quick Learner
items.content:
Dynamic Programming (DP) has a reputation for being one of the trickiest topics in algorithms. Many learners struggle not because DP is inherently complex, but because they lack a structured approach to reasoning about problems. In this blog, we’ll break DP down into that you can apply to most problems.

To make things concrete, we’ll use the Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) problem as our running example.

## 🧠 5-Step Framework to Solve DP Problems

### 1. Visualize Examples (Think in Terms of a DAG)

Almost all DP problems can be imagined as paths in a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). Each state represents a choice, and edges represent transitions to future states.

Let’s take our example array:

Try to imagine all sequences that can be formed by picking numbers in increasing order. Visualizing a few example sequences helps greatly:

  • Starting at → [3, 8] or [3, 5]
  • Starting at → [1, 8], [1, 2, 5]

Each number can go to any number that appears later in the list — this is your DAG.

### 2. Find an Appropriate Subproblem

A good DP solution boils down to defining the right subproblem.

For LIS, a natural subproblem is:

What is the longest increasing subsequence starting at index ?

What is the LIS ending at index ?

dp[i] = length of the longest increasing subsequence ending at index i

### 3. Find Relationships Among Subproblems

Now that we have , how do we compute it?

For each index , look at all previous indices :

  • If , then we can extend the subsequence ending at .
dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j]) over all j < i where arr[j] < arr[i]

If no such exists, then (the element itself).

### 4. Generalize the Relationship

dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j] for all valid j)
otherwise dp[i] = 1

This is our .

### 5. Implement by Solving Subproblems in Order

We fill the array from left to right, ensuring all values needed for have already been computed.

Here’s a full implementation:

Nice, let’s zoom all the way in on this array and see exactly what’s happening:

= length of the Longest Increasing Subsequence that ends exactly at index i

So for each position , we assume is the of the subsequence.

Why all 1’s? Because every single element alone is a subsequence of length 1.

How this is DP (subproblem view)

items.title: Telegram Bot @ChatGPTTlgrmBot Updated to GPT-5.1: New Features and Promo Code
items.author: King Triton
items.content:
OpenAI has released the new GPT-5.1, and I didn’t waste any time updating my Telegram bot @ChatGPTTlgrmBot. Now the bot responds using the latest model, which the company describes as “warmer, more intelligent, and better at following instructions.” Even FastCompany confirms: GPT-5.1 is “smarter and more conversational.” To keep up with the hype around the update, several new features have been added to the bot:

  • – The bot now runs on OpenAI’s latest model. According to OpenAI, GPT-5.1 Instant is “our most-used model, now warmer, more intelligent, and better at following instructions.” In simple terms, the responses are livelier, clearer, and sometimes friendlier.
  • – I added promo code CRTD5, which gives +3 free queries (use the /promo command). This addresses a past issue: one free query was too little, so now new users are welcomed more generously.
  • More Free Queries for New Users – Instead of 1, you now get 3 free queries on the first bot launch.
  • Convenient and Transparent Payments – Query payments still go through Telegram Stars (⭐): one star costs around 5 ₽, and 1 query = 1 star. Stars are purchased directly in Telegram. Telegram Stars are the app’s internal currency for paying for digital goods and services.

Using it remains intuitively simple:

  1. Ask a question in any language: the bot answers on any topic, just like before.
  2. If free queries run out, you can get more for free: enter /promo and use code CRTD5. This will give you +3 more free queries.
  3. Purchasing queries: enter the /buy command. Specify a number (any: 1, 5, 10, 25, etc.) or click a button in the menu. Pay via Telegram Stars (⭐) and your queries will be activated.

Honestly, the reason is simple: I love experimenting with AI and didn’t want to miss the moment. OpenAI released GPT-5.1, everyone is talking about it, so I had to follow the trend. I already had a Telegram bot on ChatGPT, and the first project articles were even written by the AI itself. Updating to GPT-5.1 is a great excuse to level up the project and test the demand.

The project repository on GitHub is open under the MIT license. Why? I see several benefits:

  • : The code (Python + Telegram Bot API + OpenAI API) is visible to everyone – a live showcase of my skills.
  • : Open code earns respect – users see there’s nothing to hide.
  • : The example may help someone build their own bot or inspire a startup.
  • : With open source, I immediately receive PRs, bug reports, and suggestions that help improve the project.

To explore all the new features, go to @ChatGPTTlgrmBot on Telegram. The first question will definitely be free (actually three). If you have questions or ideas, message @king_triton. Also, we have a Telegram channel “ChatGPT Created” – check it out for lots of fun and useful AI content.

P.S. I hope GPT-5.1 will make the bot even more enjoyable. Hugs to everyone and good luck with your new queries!

items.title: Tech With Tim: 7 Python Anti Patterns to Avoid
items.author: Vibe YouTube
items.content:
Dive into seven deceptively harmless Python habits that can turn your code into a maintenance nightmare—each one timestamped so you can skip right to your pain point. From sloppy loops to misuse of built-ins, this video warns you why “it works” isn’t always good enough and how small tweaks can save you big headaches down the road.

Along the way you’ll get a free Airia account link for playing with AI agents, a logging deep-dive to sharpen your troubleshooting, and a peek at DevLaunch’s no-fluff mentorship program that takes you from tutorials to job-ready projects.

items.title: Krish Naik: Stop Fighting with Kubernetes! Scale Python to 1000s of Machines with Coiled
items.author: Vibe YouTube
items.content:
Coiled is a lightweight cloud compute platform for Python data engineers and scientists that lets you skip Docker and Kubernetes entirely. With a single decorator or line of code, you can scale your pandas, NumPy, scikit-learn or PyTorch workloads to hundreds or thousands of machines while still using Jupyter, VS Code or PyCharm.

It spins up only when your job starts and shuts down as soon as it finishes—so you pay only for what you use. Plus, your local packages, credentials and files are automatically synced to the cloud, eliminating Docker builds and environment-file headaches.

items.title: Definition and Purpose of Buying a Corporate Website
items.author: zahra panahi
items.content:
Definition and Purpose of Buying a Corporate Website (خريد سايت شرکتي) Buying a corporate website means investing in creating an official and authoritative digital platform for a company or organization. This website, more than a simple online brochure, acts as your 24-hour showcase and digital headquarters. Its main purpose is to establish credibility, provide accurate information about services or products, introduce brand identity and values, and create a direct communication channel with customers, business partners, and investors. A professional corporate website is the first point of contact that many audiences will have with your business, and its design and content have a direct impact on their initial judgment and the level of trust they have in your brand.

  1. Different Options for “Buying” a Corporate Website

When it comes to “buying” a corporate website, there are usually three main options available to you, each with a different price and level of quality:

The Economical Option (Template-Based Sites): This method involves purchasing a ready-made WordPress theme and partially customizing it. This option is the cheapest and fastest way, but your site will not be unique and you may encounter limitations in performance and speed. The Professional and Conventional Solution (Semi-Custom WordPress Site): In this method, a website design company will design a website tailored to your needs, using WordPress and a combination of professional templates and custom development. This option strikes a good balance between cost, time, and quality and is suitable for most companies.

Advanced Solution (Custom Coded Website): For large companies or businesses with very specific needs, the site is coded from scratch using multiple programming languages. This method offers the highest level of flexibility, security, and performance, but is the most expensive and time-consuming option.

  1. Key and Essential Components of a Successful Corporate Website

A successful corporate website should include a set of essential pages and features to achieve its goals:

Professional and Responsive Design: The site’s appearance should be modern, clean, and consistent with your brand identity, and displayed correctly on all devices (mobile, tablet, and desktop). Essential pages: include the home page (general introduction), about us (story, team and company history), services/products (with full descriptions), contact us (with contact form, address and map) and news or articles section (blog) to attract the audience and improve SEO.

High-quality content: Texts should be professional, without spelling mistakes and completely clear. Using high-quality images and videos is also essential to attract attention.

Search engine optimization (SEO): The technical structure of the site should be optimized from the beginning to rank in Google so that potential customers can find you.

High speed and security: The site should load quickly and ensure the security of user information by having an SSL certificate (HTTPS protocol).

  1. The purchase process and investment perspective

The process of purchasing a corporate site usually begins with a consultation and needs assessment session to determine your goals. The design company then provides a proposal (formal proposal) including a description of the services, timing, and cost. After the agreement and signing of the contract, the design, development, content entry, testing, and finally launch phases are carried out. It is very important to look at the purchase of a corporate website as a strategic investment, not an expense. A cheap and weak website can damage your reputation and take away many opportunities from you, while a professional website will be a powerful tool for marketing, branding, and growing your business with a very high return on investment. Also, include ongoing costs such as annual renewals of hosting and domain, and support and maintenance contracts in your budget.

items.title: Kay Hayen: Nuitka Release 2.8
items.content:
• : For the “Python Build Standalone” flavor ensured that debug builds correctly recognize all their specific built-in modules, preventing potential errors. (Fixed in 2.7.2 already.)

  • : Fixed a crash when attempting to modify the RPATH of statically linked executables (e.g., from ). (Fixed in 2.7.2 already.)

  • : Updated support to correctly handle path changes in newer Conda packages and improved path normalization for robustness. (Fixed in 2.7.2 already.)

  • : Corrected handling of framework resources. Previous special handling was removed as symlinking is now default, which also resolved an issue of file duplication. (Fixed in 2.7.2 already.)

  • : Resolved an issue in debug builds where an incorrect assertion was done during the addition of distribution metadata. (Fixed in 2.7.1 already.)

  • : Corrected an issue preventing from functioning with Python versions earlier than 3.6. (Fixed in 2.7.1 already.)

  • : Prevented from crashing when was used with a built-in module. (Fixed in 2.7.1 already.)

  • : Addressed a compatibility issue where the mode for the constants blob failed with the C++ fallback. This fallback is utilized on very old GCC versions (e.g., default on ), which are generally not recommended. (Fixed in 2.7.1 already.)

  • : Resolved an assertion error that could occur in certain Python setups due to extension module suffix ordering. The issue involved incorrect calculation of the derived module name when the wrong suffix was applied (e.g., using to derive a module name like instead of just ). This was observed with Python 2 on but could potentially affect other versions with unconventional extension module configurations. (Fixed in 2.7.1 already.)

  • : Corrected the usage of an internal structure identifier that is only available in Python 3.12.1 and later versions. (Fixed in 2.7.1 already.)

  • : Prevented crashes in Python setups where importing results in a . This typically occurs in broken installations, for instance, where some packages are installed with root privileges. (Fixed in 2.7.1 already.)

  • : Implemented a workaround for data file names that previously could not be signed within app bundles. The attempt in release 2.7 to sign these files inadvertently caused a regression for cases involving illegal filenames. (Fixed in 2.7.1 already.)

  • : Addressed an issue where objects lacked the attribute. now tracks the original function as a distinct value. (Fixed in 2.7.1 already.)

  • Corrected behavior for implementations that lack a method, ensuring does not attempt to use it. (Fixed in 2.7.1 already.)

  • : Ensured compatibility for setups where the macro is unavailable. This macro is now used beyond assertions, necessitating support outside of debug mode. (Fixed in 2.7.1 already.)

  • : Resolved an issue caused by the absence of by utilizing a numeric index instead and adding a new helper function to access it. (Fixed in 2.7.1 already.)

  • : Corrected the value to accurately reflects the package in which a module is loaded, this was not the case for Python versions prior to 3.12. (Fixed in 2.7.1 already.)

  • : Further improved the plugin by preventing assertions related to empty annotations and by removing hard-coded module names for greater flexibility. (Fixed in 2.7.1 already.)

  • : For onefile mode using DLL mode, ensure all necessary environment variables are correctly set for . Previously, default Qt paths could point incorrectly near the onefile binary. (Fixed in 2.7.3 already.)

  • : Fixed an issue with where slots defined in base classes might not be correctly handled, leading to them only working for the first class that used them. (Fixed in 2.7.3 already.)

  • : Enhanced Qt binding plugin support by checking for module presence without strictly requiring metadata. This improves compatibility with environments like Homebrew or where package metadata might be absent. (Fixed in 2.7.3 already.)

  • : Ensured the target is specified during linking to prevent potential linker warnings about using an target in certain configurations. (Fixed in 2.7.3 already.)

  • : Disabled the use of static with installations, as this configuration is currently broken. (Fixed in 2.7.3 already.)

  • : Improved error handling for the option by catching cases where a description is not provided. (Fixed in 2.7.3 already.)

  • : Enhanced workarounds for , now also covering single-shot timer callbacks. (Fixed in 2.7.4 already.)

  • : Ensured that the Qt binding module is included when using accelerated mode with Qt bindings. (Fixed in 2.7.4 already.)

  • : Avoided signing through symlinks and minimized their use to prevent potential issues, especially during code signing of application bundles. (Fixed in 2.7.4 already.)

  • : Implemented path shortening for paths used in onefile DLL mode to prevent issues with long or Unicode paths. This also benefits module mode. (Fixed in 2.7.4 already.)

  • : The options nanny plugin no longer uses a deprecated option for macOS app bundles, preventing potential warnings or issues. (Fixed in 2.7.4 already.)

  • : Ensured the correct macOS target architecture is used. This particularly useful for with universal CPython binaries, to prevent compile time crashes e.g. when cross-compiling for a different architecture. (Fixed in 2.7.4 already.)

  • : Fixed a crash that occurred on if the download was rejected by the user. (Fixed in 2.7.4 already.)

  • : Improved the warning message related to macOS application icons for better clarity. (Added in 2.7.4 already.)

  • : Corrected an issue with QML plugins on when using newer versions. (Fixed in 2.7.4 already.)

  • : Fixed a memory leak where the matched value in pattern matching constructs was not being released. (Fixed in 2.7.4 already.)

  • : Fixed an issue where exception exits for larger objects, which are not optimized away, were not correctly annotated by the compiler. (Fixed in 2.7.4 already.)

  • : Corrected an issue with the automatic use of icons for applications on non-Windows if Windows icon options were used. (Fixed in 2.7.4 already.)

  • : When using DLL mode there was a load error for the DLL with MSVC 14.2 or earlier, but older MSVC is to be supported. (Fixed in 2.7.5 already.)

  • : Fix, the splash screen was showing in DLL mode twice or more; these extra copies couldn’t be stopped. (Fixed in 2.7.5 already.)

  • : Fixed an issue where data files were no longer checked for conflicts with included DLLs. The order of data file and DLL copying was restored, and macOS app signing was made a separate step to remove the order dependency. (Fixed in 2.7.6 already.)

  • : Corrected our workaround using symlinks for files that cannot be signed. When was used, as it made incorrect assumptions about the folder path. (Fixed in 2.7.6 already.)

  • : Prevented checks on onefile target specifications when not actually compiling in onefile mode, e.g. on macOS with . (Fixed in 2.7.6 already.)

  • : Improved error messages for data directory options by include the relevant part in the output. (Fixed in 2.7.6 already.)

  • : Suppressed messages from the module during compilation. (Fixed in 2.7.6 already.)

  • : Fixed an issue where descriptors for compiled methods were incorrectly exposed for Python 3.11 and 3.12. (Fixed in 2.7.7 already.)

  • : Avoided loading modules when checking for data file existence. This prevents unnecessary module loading and potential crashes in broken installations. (Fixed in 2.7.9 already.)

  • : The anti-bloat feature now operates on what should be the qualified names () instead of just function names, preventing incorrect replacements of methods with the same name in different classes. (Fixed in 2.7.9 already.)

  • : The attribute of the object was regressed in DLL mode on , pointing to the temporary DLL directory instead of the directory containing the onefile binary. (Fixed in 2.7.10 already, note that the solution in 2.7.9 had a regression.)

  • : Fixed a crash that occurred when an import attempted to go outside its package boundaries. (Fixed in 2.7.11 already.)

  • : Ignored a warning from when using self-signed certificates. (Fixed in 2.7.11 already.)

  • : Fixed an issue in DLL mode where environment variables from other onefile processes (related to temporary paths and process IDs) were not being ignored, which could lead to conflicts. (Fixed in 2.7.12 already.)

  • : Fixed a potential crash that could occur when processing an empty code body. (Fixed in 2.7.13 already.)

  • : Ensured that DLL directories created by plugins could be at the top level when necessary, improving flexibility. (Fixed in 2.7.13 already.)

  • : On , corrected an issue in DLL mode where was ; it is now properly set. (Fixed in 2.7.13 already.)

  • : Avoided a warning that appeared on newer macOS versions. (Fixed in 2.7.13 already.)

  • : Allowed another DLL to be missing for to support more setups. (Fixed in 2.7.13 already.)

  • : Corrected the existing import workaround for Python 3.12 that was incorrectly renaming existing modules of matching names into sub-modules of the currently imported module. (Fixed in 2.7.14 already.)

  • : On , ensured that the DLL search path correctly uses the proper DLL directory. (Fixed in 2.7.14 already.)

  • : Fixed a memory leak where the called object could be leaked in calls with keyword arguments following a star dict argument. (Fixed in 2.7.14 already.)

  • : Fixed an issue where was not working correctly with extension modules due to sub-interpreter changes. (Fixed in 2.7.14 already.)

  • : Corrected an issue where the process ID (PID) was not set in a timely manner, which could affect onefile operations. (Fixed in 2.7.14 already.)

  • : Fixed a crash that could occur when a function with both a star-list argument and keyword-only arguments was called without any arguments. (Fixed in 2.7.16 already.)

  • : Corrected an issue where distribution names were not checked case-insensitively, which could lead to metadata not being included. (Fixed in 2.7.16 already.)

  • : Avoid using full zlib with extern declarations but instead only the CRC32 functions we need. Otherwise conflicts with OS headers could occur.

  • : Fixed an issue where scanning for standard library dependencies was unnecessarily performed.

  • : Made the runtime query code robust against modules that in stdout during import

    This affected at least giving some warnings on Windows with mere stdout prints. We now have a marker for the start of our output that we look for and safely ignore them.

  • : Do not attempt to attach to the console when running in DLL mode. For onefile with DLL mode, this was unnecessary as the bootstrap already handles it, and for pure DLL mode, it is not desired.

  • : Removed unnecessary parent process monitoring in onefile mode, as there is no child process launched.

  • : Determine version and project name for conda packages more reliably

    It seems Anaconda is giving variables in package metadata and often no project name, so we derive it from the conda files and its meta data in those cases.

  • : Make sure the SSL certificates are found when downloading on macOS, ensuring successful downloads.

  • : Fixed an issue where console mode was not working in onefile DLL mode.

  • : Fixed an issue where was used with older can give warnings about them. This fixes building on older OSes with the system gcc.

  • : Fix, need to avoid using filenames with more than 250 chars for long module names.

    • For cache files, const files, and C files, we need to make sure, we don’t exceed the 255 char limits per path element that literally every OS has.

    • Also enhanced the check code for legal paths to cover this, so user options are covered from this errors too.

    • Moved file hashing to file operations where it makes more sense to allow module names to use hashing to provide a legal filename to refer to themselves.

  • : Fixed an issue where walking included compiled packages through the Nuitka loader could produce incorrect names in some cases.

  • : Fixed wrong calls made when checking properties during launch if it was .

  • : Fixed an issue where the segfault non-deployment disable itself before doing anything else.

  • : Fix, the warning to choose a GUI plugin for was given with plugin enabled still, which is of course not appropriate.

  • : Fix, do not recreate the build folder with a file.

    We were re-creating it as soon as we looked at what it would be, now it’s created only when asking for that to happen.

  • : Addressed compile errors for the free-threaded dictionary implementation that were introduced by necessary hot-fixes in the version 2.7.

  • : Fixed handling of generic classes and generic type declarations in Python 3.12.

  • : Fixed an issue where entitlements were not properly provided for code signing.

  • : Fixed delayed shutdown for terminal applications in onefile DLL mode.

    Was waiting for non-used child processes, which don’t exist and then the timeout for that operation, which is always happening on CTRL-C or terminal shutdown.

  • Python3.13: Fix, seems interpreter frames with None code objects exist and need to be handled as well.

  • : Fix, need to allow for package to be user provided.

  • : Avoided using non-encodable dist and build folder names.

    Some paths don’t become short, but still be non-encodable from the file system for tools. In these cases, temporary filenames are used to avoid errors from C compilers and other tools.

  • Python3.13: Fix, ignore stdlib module that might be left over from previous installs

    The module was removed during development, and if you install over an old alpha version of 3.13 a newer Python, Nuitka would crash on it.

  • : Allowed the folder for the Python Build Standalone flavor, improving compatibility.

  • : Allowed libraries for resolution to be found in all Homebrew folders and not just .

  • : Need to allow in paths to allow outside installation paths.

items.title: What is an e-commerce website and why do you need one?
items.author: zahra panahi
items.content:
1. What is an e-commerce website and why do you need one?

An e-commerce website is an online platform that allows you to sell your products or services directly to customers. This site is actually your online store that is open 24 hours a day, 7 days a week and has no geographical restrictions. Unlike a corporate website whose main purpose is to provide information, the main purpose of an e-commerce website is to conduct transactions. These websites are equipped with the necessary tools to manage products (such as adding, editing and categorizing products), process orders, manage inventory and, most importantly, receive online payments through banking portals. Buying an e-commerce website means building the main engine of your online business that can transform your sales.

  1. Main Options for “Buying” an E-Commerce Website

There are several solutions to having an e-commerce website, with different costs and levels of control:

SaaS platforms like Shopify: In this model, you “rent” a store on a monthly or annual basis. It’s very quick and easy to set up, but your control is limited and you’ll always be tied to that platform. (This option is less popular in Iran due to the limited Iranian payment gateways).(خريد سايت فروشگاهي) Using Open-Source Content Management Systems: This is the most popular and standard solution in Iran. You build a complete and powerful e-commerce website using WordPress and the WooCommerce store builder plugin. In this method, you are the full owner of your site and have a lot of flexibility for customization and development. The initial cost includes design and setup, and ongoing costs are limited to hosting and domain.

Custom coding: For very large stores with unique processes, the site is programmed from scratch. This method offers the highest level of performance, security, and flexibility, but is much more expensive and time-consuming, and is usually recommended for large-scale businesses.

  1. Vital and essential components of a successful e-commerce site

A functional e-commerce site is not just a gallery of products, but must have a set of key features to make the shopping experience enjoyable and easy for the customer:

Full product management: Ability to add products with full details (price, image, description, features, availability). Shopping cart and secure checkout: A simple and smooth process to finalize the purchase and connect to reliable bank payment gateways.

Responsive design: Perfect display of the site on mobile, because a large part of online purchases are made through smartphones.

Advanced search and filter: The ability to easily search for products and filter them based on price, brand, color, etc.

User panel: A space for customers to view their previous orders, manage addresses, and track the status of the current order.

High speed and security: A slow or insecure site quickly destroys customer trust. Having an SSL (HTTPS) certificate is an absolute must.

  1. Beyond the purchase: Costs and investment perspective

The cost of “buying a store website” is not the only initial cost. You should also consider a budget for the following: annual hosting and domain costs, technical support and maintenance costs to ensure the proper functioning of the site, and most importantly, the cost of digital marketing. Remember that building a store is just the first step; the real step is driving customers to it through SEO, online advertising, and social media. Ultimately, look at your storefront as a vital investment, not an expense. A professional and efficient online storefront can become your primary source of income and provide a tremendous return on investment, while a cheap and weak site will only waste your money and time.

items.title: Day 35: Python Morse Code Generator, Convert English Text to Morse with Full A-Z Mapping and Interactive Input
items.author: Shahrouz Nikseresht
items.content:
Welcome to Day 35 of the journey! This intermediate challenge focuses on building a simple Morse code generator that translates English text to Morse signals, supporting the full alphabet from A to Z through a dictionary lookup, while managing user input in an interactive loop for repeated conversions. It incorporates clean string processing to handle words and letters with appropriate spacing, plus a placeholder for unknown characters, making it a robust utility for text encoding. This exercise strengthens skills in dictionary usage for mappings, string manipulation for case and splits, and loop-based interactivity with exit conditions, which are essential for creating user-friendly command-line tools or encoding apps. If you're advancing from basic dicts to more integrated scripts or need practice in modular translation logic, this "Python Morse code generator" walkthrough details a function that's extensible, perhaps to include numbers or punctuation, and demonstrates real-world handling of inputs like mixed case or unsupported chars.

💡 Key Takeaways from Day 35: Morse Code Generation Function

This project centers on a core function for translation, backed by a predefined dictionary for Morse codes, and wrapped in an interactive main block for user engagement. It's a well-rounded example of data-driven processing: define a mapping, process input step-by-step, output formatted results. We'll explore it thoroughly: dictionary setup for A-Z codes, function logic with case handling, splitting, and placeholders, interactive loop for repeated use, along with discussions on design choices for efficiency, readability, and potential expansions.

1. Morse Mapping: Comprehensive A-Z Dictionary

The foundation is a static dictionary that maps each uppercase letter to its Morse equivalent:

Using adds type hints for clarity, hinting that keys and values are strings. This dict is efficient for O(1) lookups, far better than if-elif chains for 26 cases, and it's easily extensible, say by adding numbers ("0": "-----") or symbols. The codes follow international standards, with dots and dashes only, no extras like prosigns. By sticking to uppercase keys, it sets up for input normalization, ensuring consistency. This structure is reusable, perhaps in a reverse decoder function where you'd map codes back to letters.

2. Generation Function: Input Processing and Formatting

The function takes text and an optional placeholder, returning the encoded string:

Start with normalization:

This makes it case-insensitive, so "Hello" becomes "HELLO" for lookups. handles multiple spaces or tabs as one, treating the input as space-separated words.

For each char, fetches the code or defaults to "?", gracefully ignoring punctuation or numbers. The inner list collects codes, joined by single spaces for letter separation, per Morse conventions.

Double spaces distinguish words. For "SOS", it yields "... --- ...", readable and standard. The placeholder param allows customization, like "" to skip unknowns. This nested loop is clear, O(n) for text length, and avoids recursion for simplicity. Choices like split/join favor string methods over manual indexing, boosting readability without sacrificing performance for short inputs.

3. Interactive Main: User Loop with Exit

The script runs interactively under , for standalone execution:

cleans whitespace from input. The exit is case-insensitive. This loop enables multiple conversions without restarts, user-friendly for testing phrases. Emojis add visual flair, but could be toggled. It handles empty inputs by producing empty Morse, or unknowns with "?", showing thoughtful edge handling. For production, add try-except around input for robustness, but here it keeps focus on core logic.

🎯 Summary and Reflections

This Morse code generator transforms text encoding into an interactive tool, leveraging dicts for quick mappings and loops for structured output. It reinforced key principles for me:

  • : Dicts excel for static lookups, scalable to fuller char sets without code bloat.
  • : Upper and split ensure reliability, common in text processors to avoid surprises.
  • : Placeholders over errors make it forgiving, better for casual use.
  • : Separate function from main allows unit testing, like asserting "A" -> ".-".
  • : While loop with break creates a simple CLI, extensible to commands like "decode".

What stood out was its balance, intermediate yet accessible, teaching real patterns like config params (placeholder) and typing for hints. Limitations? No numbers/symbols, but adding to dict is trivial. Surprising how Morse's variable lengths (E: ".", J: ".---") highlight why fixed encodings evolved. For enhancements, add audio output via libraries like pydub, or a decoder reversing the dict.

: Use regex for input cleaning, or comprehensions: " ".join(" ".join(MORSE_MAPPING.get(c, "?") for c in word) for word in text.upper().split()). For speed on long texts, precompute, but unnecessary here. How do you handle encodings? Comment your tips!

🚀 Next Steps and Resources

Day 35 bridged data structures with user interaction, setting up for more encoding/decoding adventures. In #80DaysOfChallenges? Added numbers? Share your extension!

items.title: Krish Naik: Stop Fighting with Kubernetes! Scale Python to 1000s of Machines with Coiled
items.author: Vibe YouTube
items.content:
Ever wished spinning up hundreds of machines was as easy as adding a decorator to your Python script? Meet Coiled: a super-light cloud compute platform built for data engineers and scientists that lets you stick with pandas, NumPy, scikit-learn, PyTorch—or whatever you already love—without ever touching Docker or Kubernetes. Your code looks almost identical to local, and with one line you’re suddenly running on dozens or even thousands of cloud nodes.

When your job’s done, those machines vanish into thin air, so you only pay for what you actually use. Plus, your local packages, credentials, and files automatically sync to the cloud—no more painful Docker builds or environment.yml headaches.

items.title: Single Page Web App in Python - Part 1
items.author: Stefan Meisner Larsen
items.content:
I found myself working on yet another python web application, copying some stuff from the previous one, improving it a bit and wondering if now is the time to create that great example project to make life easier next time. I am starting this without knowing exactly where it will end, but I imagine a basic single page application with complete build and installation scripts for Linux, Docker and Kubernetes.

In this post I will create a small python project that can render a page in a browser - like Hello World for web apps. This will be the foundation for the next, soon to follow post. Stay tuned!

There are a number of web frameworks for python out there. I have stumbled into Flask a number of times. It's quite minimalistic compared to for example Django, and you're up and running in a very few lines of code.

Flask comes along with Jinja2 templating engine], another of my favorite tools.

First things first - I have googled around and experimented too, until I settled with this project layout, which is quite common I believe. If you are used to setup.py and requirements.txt this may be new for you. Welcome to the world of pyproject.toml!

I have found the following project layout to work well for me:


  single_page_python/
    README.md
    LICENSE
    .gitignore
    pyproject.toml
    src/
      single_page_python.py
      single_page_python/
        __init__.py
        server.py
        templates/
          index.html.j2

Quick walk-through of the project files

I have not experimented with other build systems than setuptools, it has worked well for me so far. Dependencies goes into "dependencies" in the project section, and in the last section there is a list of development dependencies, required to build the project. This is also where you would add pytest and other stuff that should not be part of the shipped application.

server.py - the interesting stuff :)

We start by creating a Flask app - an instance of the class Flask, stating the name of our module as argument.

Using the app.route as decorator, we can easily specify, that any request for '/' should be handled by the index() method.

The render_template() function takes the name of a jinja2 template located in 'templates' folder, relative to server.py.

The 'title' argument is a context variable, that will be passed to the jinja template. Let's take a look at that!

The naming of this file, with double extension, makes it possible to provide syntax highlighting for both Jinja and HTML (at least in vscode, I did not get it to work here). Look how the 'title' argument to render_template() is being used:

single_page_python.py - Where it all begins

This is the application entry point, the python file that you will actually be executing.

Creating a virtual environment for our project

Before installing any packages, we will create a virtual python environment ".venv" in the project root. The virtual environment is an isolated python installation, This will ensure that we don't tamper with the global python installation. On my linux system, the global python interpreter is called python3. We use the global interpreter to create the virtual environment. After creating the environment, don't forget to activate it. This is done by "sourcing" the activate script using ".":

python  venv .venv
 .venv/bin/activate

In the project root (where pyproject.tom is) install the application and the required dependencies:

python  pip 
python src/single_page_python.py
